questions.question: Prove that the sum of the angles of a triangle is 180° using parallel lines.
questions.answer: Draw a line parallel to one side of the triangle through the opposite vertex. Using alternate interior angles, show that the sum of the three angles equals 180°.

questions.question: Prove the Pythagoras theorem using a square on the sides of a right-angled triangle.
questions.answer: Construct squares on all three sides of the triangle. Show that the area of the square on the hypotenuse equals the sum of areas of squares on the other two sides.

questions.question: Prove that the medians of a triangle meet at a single point.
questions.answer: Use coordinate geometry or vector method to show that the three medians intersect at the centroid, which divides each median in 2:1 ratio.

questions.question: Prove that the angle bisectors of a triangle meet at the incenter.
questions.answer: Show that the intersection point of two angle bisectors is equidistant from all sides, hence the third bisector passes through the same point, which is the incenter.

questions.question: Prove that the perpendicular bisectors of the sides meet at the circumcenter.
questions.answer: Show that the intersection point of two perpendicular bisectors is equidistant from the vertices, so the third bisector passes through it, forming the circumcenter.

questions.question: Prove that the altitudes of a triangle meet at the orthocenter.
questions.answer: Use the properties of perpendicular lines and triangle angles to show that the three altitudes intersect at a single point called the orthocenter.

questions.question: Derive Heron's formula for the area of a triangle.
questions.answer: Start from area = 1/2 × base × height, express height in terms of sides using Pythagoras theorem, simplify to √[s(s−a)(s−b)(s−c)] with s = (a+b+c)/2.

questions.question: Prove the properties of an equilateral triangle regarding medians, altitudes, and bisectors.
questions.answer: Show that all medians, altitudes, and angle bisectors coincide in an equilateral triangle using symmetry and congruence arguments.

questions.question: Prove the similarity criteria AA, SAS, and SSS for triangles.
questions.answer: Use corresponding angles equality and side proportionality to derive each criterion showing that triangles are similar under each condition.

questions.question: Prove that the ratio of areas of two similar triangles is equal to the square of the ratio of their corresponding sides.
questions.answer: Use area formula 1/2 × base × height and similarity proportionality to show that (area1/area2) = (side1/side2)².

questions.question: Prove that the centroid divides each median in 2:1 ratio.
questions.answer: Using coordinate geometry, place triangle vertices, find median equations, calculate intersection, and show distance ratios along median = 2:1.

questions.question: Prove that the incenter is equidistant from all sides of a triangle.
questions.answer: Use angle bisector theorem and perpendicular distances from incenter to sides to prove equality.

questions.question: Prove that the circumcenter is equidistant from all vertices of a triangle.
questions.answer: Using perpendicular bisectors, show that the intersection point has equal distance to all vertices.

questions.question: Prove that the medians of a triangle divide it into six smaller triangles of equal area.
questions.answer: Draw all medians, show that each smaller triangle has equal base and height proportions, hence equal areas.

questions.question: Prove the Pythagorean theorem using similar triangles method.
questions.answer: Draw an altitude from the right angle to hypotenuse, identify smaller triangles similar to original triangle, use proportionality to derive a² + b² = c².

questions.question: Prove that the sum of lengths of any two medians is greater than the third median.
questions.answer: Use triangle inequality theorem on the triangle formed by medians to prove this property.

questions.question: Prove that in a right-angled triangle, the centroid lies inside the triangle.
questions.answer: Using coordinate method, place triangle vertices, calculate centroid coordinates, show it lies within the triangle bounds.

questions.question: Prove that the circumcenter of a right-angled triangle lies at the midpoint of the hypotenuse.
questions.answer: Use perpendicular bisectors of sides, show intersection at midpoint of hypotenuse using distance formula.

questions.question: Prove that the orthocenter lies inside for acute triangles and outside for obtuse triangles.
questions.answer: Using altitudes and angle properties, show intersection point location inside acute and outside obtuse triangles.

questions.question: Prove the inequality that the sum of any two sides of a triangle is greater than the third side.
questions.answer: Consider triangle ABC, apply geometric arguments to show AB + BC > AC, BC + AC > AB, AC + AB > BC.

questions.question: Prove that in an isosceles triangle, the angles opposite equal sides are equal.
questions.answer: Use congruence of triangles formed by drawing altitude from vertex opposite base to base.

questions.question: Prove that the external angle of a triangle is equal to the sum of the two opposite interior angles.
questions.answer: Using linear pair property and angle sum property, show that external angle = sum of two opposite interior angles.

questions.question: Prove that in similar triangles, corresponding medians are proportional to corresponding sides.
questions.answer: Use similarity ratios, apply them to medians using triangle properties to show proportionality.

questions.question: Prove that the line joining midpoints of two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side and half its length.
questions.answer: Use coordinate geometry or vector method to show midpoint line parallel and proportional in length.
answer_outline: Use geometry or vector to prove property

questions.question: Prove that the sum of the squares of medians of a triangle equals 3/4 the sum of squares of sides.
questions.answer: Use median formula in terms of sides and simplify: m_a² + m_b² + m_c² = 3/4 (a² + b² + c²).
answer_outline: Express medians in terms of sides
